headline: Agent memory and persistent state became the defining theme of the month across coding agents and production deployments.
body: Agent infrastructure dominated the month, with persistent memory and state the recurring theme: talks argued that coding agents fail without durable context [4], that session logs should anchor agent identity over orchestration layers [6], and that notes and documents can be converted into agent memory representations [0]. Production evaluation surfaced as a parallel concern, with Meta detailing continuous eval pipelines, drift detection, and human-in-the-loop systems for agentic workflows at scale [11] and OpenGov covering A2A protocol and monitoring in deployed government agents [3]. Local and open agentic coding models advanced through the Ornith family from DeepReinforce, spanning 9B to 397B parameters with MIT licensing and GGUF builds [1]. On the breakout side, benchmark, RLHF, multi-step workflows, and optimal transport all moved sharply, alongside new technique entrants including primitive-action decomposition, optimization bounds, the Discrete Hartley Transform, and Green's functions.

cited_items.title: Turn 10,994 Notes Into Memory - Paul Iusztin, Decoding AI & Louis-François Bouchard, Towards AI
Ornith (35B,9B) + Hermes,Zed: THE FULLY PRIVATE LOCAL AGENT is ACTUALLY HERE!
Agents in Production: How OpenGov Built and Scaled OG Assist - Gabe De Mesa, OpenGov
A Genius With Amnesia - Victor Savkin, Nx
The Log Is The Agent - Ishaan Sehgal, Omnara
Production Evals For Agentic AI Systems - Nishant Gupta, Meta Superintelligence Labs
